content.body: Diffusion is the net movement of particles from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration, down a concentration gradient. It occurs in both gases and liquids, and requires NO energy (passive process). Diffusion is vital for gas exchange in lungs, oxygen entering cells, and CO₂ leaving cells.

Key terms:
Concentration gradient: difference in concentration between two regions
Net movement: overall direction of diffusion (high → low)
Equilibrium: when concentrations are equal — particles still move but NET movement = 0

Rate of diffusion is faster when:
• Concentration gradient is steeper (bigger difference)
• Temperature is higher (particles have more energy)
• Distance is shorter
• Surface area is larger
• Particles are smaller

content.title: Examples of Diffusion
content.body:
1. In the lungs (alveoli):
   • O₂ diffuses from alveoli (high O₂) → blood (low O₂)
   • CO₂ diffuses from blood (high CO₂) → alveoli (low CO₂)

2. In body cells:
   • O₂ diffuses from blood → cells (used in respiration)
   • CO₂ diffuses from cells → blood

3. Smell of perfume spreading across a room
4. Food colouring spreading in water
5. Glucose from gut lumen → blood

Adaptations for efficient diffusion in the lungs:
• Alveoli: large total surface area (~70 m² in humans!)
• Alveoli walls: very thin (one cell thick) — short diffusion distance
• Rich capillary blood supply: maintains steep concentration gradient
• Alveoli are moist — gases dissolve for diffusion

Fick's Law: Rate of diffusion ∝ (surface area × concentration difference) / thickness of membrane

questions.question: Diffusion is the movement of particles from:
questions.answer: High to low concentration
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the net movement of particles down the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration).

questions.question: Does diffusion require energy?
options: No, it is a passive process
questions.answer: No, it is a passive process
questions.explanation: Diffusion is passive — it requires no energy input. Particles move spontaneously down the concentration gradient.

questions.question: In the lungs, O₂ diffuses from:
questions.answer: Alveoli to blood
questions.explanation: Alveoli have high O₂ (from inhaled air). Blood returning from the body has low O₂. So O₂ diffuses from alveoli → blood.

questions.question: What makes alveoli efficient for diffusion?
options: Large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply
They are connected to the heart
questions.answer: Large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply
questions.explanation: These three features maximise diffusion rate according to Fick's Law.

questions.question: Diffusion rate increases if:
questions.answer: Surface area increases
questions.explanation: Greater surface area provides more space for diffusion to occur — increasing the rate.

questions.question: When diffusion equilibrium is reached:
options: Net movement stops but particles still move randomly
All particles move to one side
questions.answer: Net movement stops but particles still move randomly
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, concentrations are equal. Particles still move randomly, but net directional movement is zero.

questions.question: Glucose absorbed in the small intestine enters the blood by:
options: Diffusion (from high conc. in gut to blood)
questions.answer: Diffusion (from high conc. in gut to blood)
questions.explanation: After digestion, glucose concentration in the gut lumen is high. It diffuses down its concentration gradient into the blood.

questions.question: Fick's Law states that diffusion rate is proportional to:
options: Surface area × concentration difference / membrane thickness
questions.answer: Surface area × concentration difference / membrane thickness
questions.explanation: Fick's Law: Rate ∝ (Surface area × concentration difference) / thickness of exchange membrane.

questions.question: Why does CO₂ diffuse from cells into the blood?
options: Blood has more CO₂ than cells
Cells have more CO₂ (from respiration) than blood
questions.answer: Cells have more CO₂ (from respiration) than blood
questions.explanation: Respiration produces CO₂ in cells, making cell CO₂ concentration high. Blood has low CO₂ — so CO₂ diffuses from cells into blood.

questions.question: The smell of cooking reaches all parts of a house. This is:
options: Diffusion of scent particles through air
questions.answer: Diffusion of scent particles through air
questions.explanation: Scent molecules diffuse from high concentration (kitchen) to low concentration (rest of house) — a real-world example of gaseous diffusion.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Osmosis is the special case of diffusion involving water. It is the net movement of water molecules from a region of high water concentration (dilute solution) to a region of low water concentration (concentrated solution) through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE membrane. Osmosis is crucial for plant turgor pressure, kidney function, and cell volume control.

Partially permeable membrane:
• Allows water (small molecules) to pass through
• Does NOT allow solute (large molecules like glucose, starch) to pass through

Water potential:
• Pure water has highest water potential (ψ = 0)
• Adding solute LOWERS water potential
• Water moves from HIGH to LOW water potential (= high to low water concentration)

content.title: Osmosis in Plant Cells
content.body:
TURGID cell (healthy): water has entered by osmosis → cell is firm/swollen → cell wall prevents bursting (turgor pressure pushes outward)

FLACCID cell: cell has lost water → limp/wilted → lower turgor pressure

PLASMOLYSIS: cell in very concentrated solution → cell membrane pulls away from cell wall → cell cannot function

Plants in dry soil: soil is more concentrated → water moves OUT of roots by osmosis → plant wilts

content.title: Osmosis in Animal Cells
content.body:
In HYPOTONIC solution (more dilute than cell): water enters cell → cell SWELLS → may BURST (lysis)

In HYPERTONIC solution (more concentrated than cell): water leaves cell → cell SHRIVELS (crenation)

In ISOTONIC solution (same concentration as cell): no net movement → cell stays the same

Blood plasma is kept isotonic with red blood cells — this is why saline drips use 0.9% salt solution.

questions.question: Osmosis is the movement of:
options: Water molecules through a partially permeable membrane
questions.answer: Water molecules through a partially permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is specifically the net movement of water through a partially permeable membrane.

questions.question: Water moves by osmosis from:
questions.answer: Dilute to concentrated solution
questions.explanation: Water moves from where it is in higher concentration (dilute solution) to lower concentration (concentrated solution).

questions.question: A turgid plant cell has:
options: Gained water and is firm due to turgor pressure
questions.answer: Gained water and is firm due to turgor pressure
questions.explanation: Turgid = swollen with water. Water has entered by osmosis, creating turgor pressure against the cell wall — keeping the plant firm.

questions.question: Plasmolysis occurs when:
options: Too much water enters a cell
Cell is placed in very concentrated solution — membrane pulls from wall
questions.answer: Cell is placed in very concentrated solution — membrane pulls from wall
questions.explanation: In hypertonic solution, water leaves by osmosis. The cell contents shrink so much the membrane peels away from the wall — plasmolysis.

questions.question: A red blood cell placed in distilled water will:
questions.answer: Swell and may burst
questions.explanation: Distilled water is hypotonic (more dilute than the cell contents). Water enters by osmosis, causing the cell to swell and potentially burst (lysis).

questions.question: Hospital saline drips use 0.9% NaCl because:
options: It is isotonic with blood — no osmosis disruption
questions.answer: It is isotonic with blood — no osmosis disruption
questions.explanation: 0.9% NaCl has the same solute concentration as blood plasma — no net osmosis occurs, so cells are not damaged.

questions.question: What passes through a partially permeable membrane?
options: Small water molecules freely; large solutes cannot pass
questions.answer: Small water molecules freely; large solutes cannot pass
questions.explanation: Partially permeable membranes allow small molecules (water) through but block large solute molecules like glucose and starch.

questions.question: Osmosis is a type of:
questions.answer: Special case of diffusion (water only)
questions.explanation: Osmosis is diffusion specifically of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane.

questions.question: A wilting plant can be restored by:
options: Watering it (water enters roots by osmosis)
questions.answer: Watering it (water enters roots by osmosis)
questions.explanation: Water in the soil is more dilute than cell contents. Water enters by osmosis restoring turgor pressure and making the plant firm again.

questions.question: Red blood cell placed in concentrated salt solution will:
questions.answer: Shrivel (crenation)
questions.explanation: Hypertonic solution has lower water potential than the cell. Water leaves by osmosis, causing the cell to shrivel — this is called crenation.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Active transport moves substances against a concentration gradient (from low to high concentration). It requires energy (ATP) and carrier proteins. Unlike diffusion and osmosis, it can work 'uphill'.

Comparison:
Diffusion: high → low conc. No energy needed.
Osmosis: high water → low water conc. No energy.
Active transport: low → HIGH conc. REQUIRES ATP and carrier proteins.

Examples:
• Root hair cells absorb mineral ions from soil (low conc soil → high conc root)
• Kidney tubules reabsorb glucose from filtrate
• Nerve cells maintaining Na⁺/K⁺ balance

content.title: Glucose Absorption in Small Intestine
content.body:
After digestion, glucose concentration in intestine may fall below blood concentration.
Active transport pumps glucose from intestine into blood against gradient.
This ensures all glucose is absorbed even when conc is lower than blood.
Energy from respiration (ATP) powers the protein pumps.

questions.question: Active transport moves substances...
questions.answer: Against a concentration gradient
questions.explanation: Active transport moves substances from low to HIGH concentration — against the gradient — requiring energy.

questions.question: Energy for active transport comes from...
questions.answer: ATP produced by respiration
questions.explanation: Active transport requires ATP (adenosine triphosphate) — the cell's energy currency produced by cellular respiration.

questions.question: Which process requires carrier proteins AND energy?
questions.answer: Active transport
questions.explanation: Active transport needs both specific carrier proteins (to bind and move solutes) and ATP energy.

questions.question: Mineral ions enter root hair cells by...
questions.answer: Active transport
questions.explanation: Mineral ions are at LOW concentration in soil but needed at HIGH concentration inside root cells — requires active transport.

questions.question: The kidney uses active transport to...
options: Reabsorb useful glucose from the filtrate
questions.answer: Reabsorb useful glucose from the filtrate
questions.explanation: Glucose in kidney filtrate is actively transported back into the blood — all glucose is normally recovered.

questions.question: A cell poisoned with cyanide (which stops ATP production) would be unable to...
questions.answer: Perform active transport
questions.explanation: Active transport requires ATP. If ATP production is blocked, active transport stops — diffusion and osmosis continue as they're passive.

questions.question: What happens to active transport if a cell runs out of oxygen?
questions.answer: Decreases or stops (less ATP made)
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen to make ATP. Less oxygen → less ATP → active transport slows/stops.

questions.question: Active transport is described as 'uphill' because...
options: It works against the concentration gradient
It requires energy like climbing a hill
questions.answer: Both B and C
questions.explanation: Like climbing uphill requires energy, active transport works against the concentration gradient and requires ATP energy.

questions.question: Na⁺/K⁺ pump in nerve cells works by...
options: Active transport of Na⁺ out and K⁺ in
questions.answer: Active transport of Na⁺ out and K⁺ in
questions.explanation: The sodium-potassium pump actively moves 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP — maintaining concentration gradients for nerve impulses.

questions.question: Surface area to volume ratio affects rate of diffusion because...
options: Larger SA:Vol → faster diffusion into/out of cell
questions.answer: Larger SA:Vol → faster diffusion into/out of cell
questions.explanation: Large SA:Vol ratio means short diffusion distances — materials reach all parts of cell quickly. Small cells and flat shapes have high SA:Vol.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Diffusion & Osmosis. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Diffusion & Osmosis concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Diffusion & Osmosis builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Diffusion & Osmosis. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Diffusion & Osmosis. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Diffusion & Osmosis include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Diffusion & Osmosis. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Diffusion & Osmosis. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Diffusion & Osmosis concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Diffusion & Osmosis builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Diffusion & Osmosis. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Diffusion & Osmosis. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Diffusion & Osmosis include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Diffusion & Osmosis. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
